title: Rheumatoid Arthritis
authors: Ankoor Shah, E. William St. Clair

items.title: Importance of Early Diagnosis and Aggressive Treatment in RA
items.content: Early diagnosis and aggressive treatment are crucial in rheumatoid arthritis (RA) to prevent joint damage, functional disability, and extraarticular manifestations. The advancements in diagnostic tools like ACPA testing and imaging modalities have improved early detection and monitoring.
keyPoints: RA is a chronic inflammatory disease characterized by symmetric, erosive polyarthritis.
Early diagnosis and aggressive treatment prevent articular cartilage and bone destruction.
Extraarticular manifestations include fatigue, subcutaneous nodules, lung involvement, pericarditis, peripheral neuropathy, vasculitis, and hematologic abnormalities.
Testing for serum antibodies to anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA) and rheumatoid factor is valuable in diagnosis.
Advances in imaging modalities assist in detecting joint inflammation and monitoring disease progression.
Early referral to a rheumatologist for confirmation of diagnosis and initiation of therapy improves outcomes.

items.title: Role of Biologic and Targeted Synthetic Therapies in RA Management
items.content: The efficacy of biologic and targeted synthetic disease-modifying therapies has highlighted the importance of cellular and inflammatory mediators in RA pathogenesis. These treatments have significantly improved patient outcomes by reducing disease activity and achieving remission.
keyPoints: Biologic and targeted synthetic therapies are effective in modifying RA progression.
These therapies illuminate new disease-related genes, environmental interactions, and molecular pathways.
Achieving low disease activity and remission is now possible for most patients with RA.
The past 20 years have seen remarkable improvements in RA outcomes due to expanded therapeutic options.

items.title: RA Incidence and Early Symptoms
items.content: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) incidence peaks between ages 25-55, with symptoms often involving joint inflammation. Patients typically experience early morning stiffness lasting over an hour that improves with activity.
keyPoints: Incidence increases between 25-55 years of age and plateaus until 75.
Early morning joint stiffness >1 h easing with physical activity.
Initial joint involvement can be monoarticular, oligoarticular (≤4 joints), or polyarticular (>5 joints) in a symmetric pattern.

items.title: RA Joint Involvement and Deformities
items.content: In established RA, wrists, MCP, and PIP joints are most affected. Common deformities include ulnar deviation and swan-neck or boutonnière deformities due to tendon damage.
keyPoints: Most frequently involved joints: wrists, MCP, and PIP.
Common deformities: ulnar deviation, swan-neck, boutonnière.
Flexor tenosynovitis can lead to decreased grip strength and trigger fingers.

items.title: Extraarticular Manifestations of RA
items.content: Up to 40% of RA patients develop extraarticular manifestations, often in those with early disability or positive RF/ACPA. Common manifestations include subcutaneous nodules and interstitial lung disease.
keyPoints: Up to 40% may develop extraarticular manifestations.
Higher risk with cigarette smoking history, early physical disability, and positive RF/ACPA.
Common manifestations: subcutaneous nodules, Sjögren's disease, ILD.

items.title: Recognizing Systemic Inflammation in Rheumatoid Arthritis
items.content: Constitutional symptoms such as weight loss, fever, fatigue, malaise, and depression can precede joint symptoms in RA. A fever >38.3°C (101°F) during the clinical course should raise suspicion for systemic vasculitis or infection.
keyPoints: Constitutional symptoms include weight loss, fever, fatigue, malaise, depression, and cachexia.
Fever >38.3°C (101°F) suggests possible systemic vasculitis or infection.
items.reference: Rheumatoid Arthritis · CONSTITUTIONAL | NODULES | SJÖGREN'S SYNDROME | PULMONARY

items.title: Understanding Subcutaneous Nodules in RA Patients
items.content: Subcutaneous nodules occur in 30–40% of RA patients, especially those with high disease activity, shared epitope (SE), positive serum RF, and joint erosions. They are firm, nontender, and can develop in areas subject to trauma or irritation.
keyPoints: Nodules occur in 30–40% of RA patients.
More common with high disease activity, shared epitope (SE), positive serum RF, and joint erosions.
Firm, nontender nodules develop in areas like the forearm, sacral prominences, and Achilles tendon.
items.reference: Rheumatoid Arthritis · CONSTITUTIONAL | NODULES | SJÖGREN'S SYNDROME | PULMONARY

items.title: Identifying Pulmonary Manifestations of RA
items.content: RA can cause pleuritis with pleural effusion and interstitial lung disease (ILD). Pleuritis presents with chest pain, dyspnea, and a friction rub. ILD symptoms include dry cough and progressive shortness of breath, diagnosed via high-resolution CT showing ground-glass opacities.
keyPoints: Pleuritis is the most common pulmonary manifestation in RA.
ILD occurs in up to 12% of RA patients, with a restrictive pattern on pulmonary function tests.
High-resolution CT shows ground-glass opacities for ILD diagnosis.
items.reference: Rheumatoid Arthritis · CONSTITUTIONAL | NODULES | SJÖGREN'S SYNDROME | PULMONARY

items.title: Cardiac Involvement in RA: Subclinical Nature and Detection Methods
items.content: In rheumatoid arthritis (RA), cardiac involvement is most frequently seen in the pericardium. Despite this, clinical manifestations of pericarditis are rare, occurring in less than 10% of patients with detectable pericardial involvement via echocardiogram or autopsy. Asymptomatic pericardial effusions may be present in up to 20% of RA patients on echocardiography. Cardiomyopathy can also occur subclinically and is identified through imaging techniques like echocardiography or cardiac MRI.
keyPoints: Pericarditis clinical manifestations occur in <10% of RA patients despite detectable pericardial involvement in nearly one-half of cases.
Asymptomatic pericardial effusions may be present in up to 20% of RA patients on echocardiography.
Cardiomyopathy may result from necrotizing or granulomatous myocarditis, coronary artery disease, or diastolic dysfunction and can be subclinical.
Echocardiography and cardiac MRI are crucial for detecting subclinical cardiac involvement.

items.title: Rheumatoid Vasculitis: Clinical Presentation and Treatment
items.content: Rheumatoid vasculitis typically occurs in patients with long-standing RA, positive serum RF or ACPA, and hypocomplementemia. Its incidence has decreased to less than 1% of patients. Cutaneous signs include petechiae, purpura, digital infarcts, gangrene, livedo reticularis, and severe cases may present with large, painful lower extremity ulcerations. These ulcers can be treated with immunosuppressive agents and skin grafting in severe cases. Sensorimotor polyneuropathies like mononeuritis multiplex are associated with systemic rheumatoid vasculitis, presenting with new onset numbness, tingling, or focal muscle weakness.
keyPoints: Rheumatoid vasculitis occurs in patients with long-standing RA, positive serum RF or ACPA, and hypocomplementemia.
Incidence has decreased to <1% of patients.
Cutaneous signs include petechiae, purpura, digital infarcts, gangrene, livedo reticularis, and large painful lower extremity ulcerations.
Vasculitic ulcers may be treated with immunosuppressive agents and skin grafting in severe cases.
Sensorimotor polyneuropathies like mononeuritis multiplex can present with numbness, tingling, or focal muscle weakness.

items.title: Increased Risk of Lymphoma in RA Patients
items.content: RA patients have a significantly higher risk of developing lymphoma compared to the general population, particularly diffuse large B-cell lymphoma. This risk is further elevated with high disease activity or Felty's syndrome.
keyPoints: Two- to fourfold increased risk of lymphoma in RA patients
Most common type: diffuse large B-cell lymphoma
Risk increases with high disease activity or Felty's syndrome
items.reference: Rheumatoid Arthritis · LYMPHOMA | ASSOCIATED CONDITIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Cardiovascular Disease and Osteoporosis in RA Patients
items.content: RA patients face a higher incidence of cardiovascular disease, including coronary artery disease, carotid atherosclerosis, and congestive heart failure. They also have nearly double the incidence rate of osteoporosis compared to age- and sex-matched populations.
keyPoints: Cardiovascular disease is the most common cause of death in RA patients
Twofold higher rate of congestive heart failure in RA patients
Osteoporosis incidence nearly double that of healthy population
Prevalence of osteoporosis approximately one-third in postmenopausal women with RA
items.reference: Rheumatoid Arthritis · LYMPHOMA | ASSOCIATED CONDITIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Genetic Risk Factors and Shared Epitope in RA
items.content: The genetic predisposition for rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is significantly influenced by the presence of specific alleles within the major histocompatibility complex (MHC), particularly those encoding MHC class II molecules. The shared epitope (SE), a sequence at positions 70–74 in HLA-DRB1, increases RA risk substantially.
keyPoints: First-degree relatives have a 2–10 times higher likelihood of developing RA compared to the general population.
Heritability estimates for RA range from 40 to 60%, potentially higher in ACPA-positive patients.
SE alleles increase RA risk fourfold with one allele and eightfold with two alleles.
Over 90% of RA patients express at least one SE variant, such as HLA-DRB1 ^{0401}, ^{0101}, ^{0404}, ^{1001}, or ^{0901}.
HLA-DRB1 ^{1301} and to a lesser extent ^{1302} confer protection from ACPA-positive RA.

items.title: Geographic Variation in HLA-DRB1 Alleles Associated with RA
items.content: The susceptibility to rheumatoid arthritis and the specific HLA-DRB1 alleles associated with it vary geographically, reflecting differences in disease severity and allele prevalence across populations.
keyPoints: In Greece, RA is milder and linked to the '0101 SE allele.
Northern Europeans predominantly carry '0401 or '0404 alleles (~50–70%).
Asian populations (Japanese, Koreans, Chinese) are associated with '0405 and '0901 alleles.
Native American groups like Pima and Tlingit Indians have a high RA prevalence linked to the '1042 SE allele.
Risk conferred by these alleles is generally lower in African and Hispanic Americans compared to Europeans.

items.title: Non-MHC Genetic Contributions to RA Susceptibility
items.content: Genome-wide association studies have identified over 100 non-MHC loci contributing modestly to RA risk, with variations among ethnic groups and implications for other autoimmune diseases.
keyPoints: GWAS has identified >100 non-MHC loci associated with RA, each having a modest effect on risk.
These loci also contribute to the risk of other autoimmune diseases like type 1 diabetes mellitus, systemic lupus erythematosus, and multiple sclerosis.
Non-HLA associations are primarily described in ACPA-positive disease, but some loci are unique to ACPA-negative disease.
Risk alleles vary among ethnic groups and mostly reside in genes regulating immune response.
Currently identified risk alleles account for ~5% of the genetic risk, indicating potential contributions from rare variants or other DNA variant classes.

items.title: Role of PTPN22 in RA Risk and ACPA-Positive Disease
items.content: The gene encoding protein tyrosine phosphatase non-receptor 22 (PTPN22) is a significant non-MHC genetic factor contributing to rheumatoid arthritis (RA). It varies in frequency among European populations (3–10%) but is absent in East Asian ancestry. The risk allele of PTPN22 leads to a gain-of-function, potentially causing abnormal thymic selection of autoreactive T and B cells, and is associated exclusively with ACPA-positive RA.
keyPoints: PTPN22 gene frequency: 3–10% in European populations
Absent in East Asian ancestry
Associated exclusively with ACPA-positive RA

items.title: Epigenetic Mechanisms and Their Role in RA Pathogenesis
items.content: Epigenetics, involving heritable changes that affect gene expression without altering DNA sequence, plays a crucial role in rheumatoid arthritis. Key epigenetic mechanisms include posttranslational histone modifications and DNA methylation. These mechanisms contribute to disease etiology, chronic inflammation perpetuation, and severity. Differences in DNA methylation patterns are observed between RA patients and healthy controls, including monozygotic twins, and those with osteoarthritis. Epigenetics may explain the increased risk of ACPA-positive RA associated with cigarette smoking.
keyPoints: Epigenetic mechanisms: histone modifications, DNA methylation
DNA methylation differences between RA patients and controls
Higher DNA methylation in smokers with ACPA-positive RA

items.title: Impact of Smoking on Rheumatoid Arthritis Risk
items.content: Cigarette smoking significantly increases the risk of developing rheumatoid arthritis (RA), especially in individuals with certain genetic predispositions. The interaction between smoking and MHC risk alleles is particularly notable, leading to a dramatic increase in RA risk.
keyPoints: Smoking confers a relative risk for RA of 1.5–3.5 times.
Risk increases synergistically with MHC risk alleles: classic SE alleles alone increase likelihood by four- to sixfold; combined with smoking, this risk escalates to 20- to 40-fold.
Women who smoke have nearly a 2.5 times greater risk of RA.
Smoking-related RA risk persists up to 15 years after cessation.
Risk from smoking is almost exclusively related to RF and ACPA-positive disease.

items.title: Role of Epstein-Barr Virus in Rheumatoid Arthritis
items.content: Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) has been associated with rheumatoid arthritis, as evidenced by higher titers of IgG antibodies against EBV antigens and the presence of EBV DNA in synovial fluid and cells. However, this association is circumstantial, and infection cannot be directly implicated as a causative factor.
keyPoints: Higher titers of IgG antibodies against EBV antigens are observed in RA patients compared to the general population.
EBV DNA found in synovial fluid and cells of RA patients.
Evidence for links between EBV and RA is largely circumstantial.

items.title: Microbial Dysbiosis and Rheumatoid Arthritis Development
items.content: Dysbiosis in the oral and gut microbiomes may predispose individuals to rheumatoid arthritis. Periodontitis, particularly involving Porphyromonas gingivalis, is linked to ACPA-positive RA through mechanisms such as citrullination by peptidyl arginine deiminase.
keyPoints: Link between ACPA-positive RA and cigarette smoking, periodontal disease, and oral microbiome.
Porphyromonas gingivalis is implicated due to its unique enzyme that induces ACPA through citrullination.
Circulating antibodies to P. gingivalis are associated with RA and at-risk first-degree relatives.
Prevotella copri enrichment observed in early untreated RA and 'at-risk' populations.

items.title: Synovial Inflammation and Pannus Formation in RA
items.content: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) involves synovial inflammation and proliferation, leading to the formation of pannus. This thickened cellular membrane is composed mainly of fibroblast-like synoviocytes and granulation tissue, which invades underlying cartilage and bone.
keyPoints: Synovial lining hyperplasia leads to pannus formation.
Pannus consists of multiple layers of fibroblast-like synoviocytes and reactive fibrovascular tissue.
The inflammatory infiltrate includes T cells (30–50%), B cells, plasma cells, dendritic cells, mast cells, and granulocytes.

items.title: Role of Osteoclasts in Bone Erosions in RA
items.content: In RA, osteoclasts mediate structural damage to bone at the pannus-bone interface. These multinucleated cells form resorption lacunae, leading to erosions typically at radial sites of MCP joints.
keyPoints: Osteoclasts appear at the pannus-bone interface and create resorption lacunae.
Bone erosions develop at radial sites of MCP joints near tendon and ligament insertions.
Periarticular osteopenia is associated with inflammation-induced thinning of bony trabeculae.

items.title: Cartilage Degradation in RA
items.content: RA leads to cartilage degradation, initially near the synovial pannus. This involves a loss of proteoglycans, particularly in superficial zones adjacent to synovial fluid.
keyPoints: Initial cartilage degradation occurs near the synovial pannus.
Loss of proteoglycan is most evident in superficial cartilage zones.
Cartilage matrix degradation may also occur in perichondrocytic zones and regions adjacent to subchondral bone.

items.title: Role of Autoantibodies in Preclinical RA
items.content: Autoantibodies such as RF and ACPA can be detected years before the clinical onset of rheumatoid arthritis, indicating a breakdown in self-tolerance.
keyPoints: RF and ACPA are autoantibodies found in sera long before RA symptoms appear.
ACPA targets deaminated peptides due to posttranslational modification by PADI4.
ACPA recognizes citrulline-containing regions of various matrix proteins like filaggrin, keratin, fibrinogen, and vimentin.
Higher levels of ACPA are present in joint fluid compared to serum.

items.title: Environmental Triggers and RA Pathogenesis
items.content: Smoking may synergize with genetic factors to induce citrullination of proteins, potentially leading to immune complex formation and joint inflammation in RA.
keyPoints: Smokers show higher protein citrullination in bronchoalveolar fluid than non-smokers.
Long-term tobacco smoke exposure might increase PADI expression, creating neoepitopes that induce self-reactivity.
This process can lead to immune complex formation and subsequent joint inflammation.

items.title: CD4+ T Cells in RA Pathogenesis
items.content: CD4+ T cells are crucial in the pathogenesis of RA, as they interact with MHC class II molecules and contribute to chronic inflammation.
keyPoints: CD4+ T cells bind to invariant sites on MHC class II molecules, stabilizing the activation complex.
The shared epitope (SE) on MHC class II is a risk factor for RA, implicating CD4+ T-cell involvement.
CD4+ memory T cells are enriched in synovial tissue of RA patients.
Animal models show that CD4+ T cells are important in initiating arthritis.
Some T cell-directed therapies have shown clinical efficacy in RA.

items.title: Role of T Helper Cells in RA Pathogenesis
items.content: T helper cells are crucial in the pathogenesis of rheumatoid arthritis (RA) through their differentiation into various subsets, each producing distinct cytokines that drive inflammation and joint destruction.
keyPoints: CD4+ T-cell activation requires two signals: T-cell receptor binding to peptide-MHC and CD28 binding to CD80/86.
T helper cells differentiate into TH1, TH2, and TH17 subsets with specific cytokine profiles.
TH1 cells produce IFN-γ, lymphotoxin β, and TNF-α; TH2 cells secrete IL-4, IL-5, IL-6, IL-10, and IL-13.
TH17 cells secrete proinflammatory mediators including IL-17, IL-21, IL-22, TNF-α, IL-26, IL-6, and GM-CSF.
IL-17 plays a significant role in promoting joint inflammation and cartilage destruction.

items.title: Regulatory T Cells as Negative Regulators in RA
items.content: Regulatory T cells (Tregs) are essential for counterbalancing immune-mediated inflammatory responses, characterized by CD25 expression and FOXP3 transcription factor.
keyPoints: Tregs suppress immune-mediated inflammation through contact with other immune cells and secretion of inhibitory cytokines like TGF-β, IL-10, and IL-35.
They can suppress TH1, TH2, and TH17 responses but their role in RA is inconclusive.

items.title: Macrophage and Cytokine Involvement in RA
items.content: RA is often considered a macrophage-driven disease, with these cells being the primary source of proinflammatory cytokines that contribute to synovial inflammation.
keyPoints: Key proinflammatory cytokines from macrophages include TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6, IL-12, IL-15, IL-18, and IL-23.

TNF-α upregulates adhesion molecules on endothelial cells, promotes leukocyte influx, activates synovial fibroblasts, stimulates angiogenesis, sensitizes pain receptors, and drives osteoclastogenesis.
Synovial fibroblasts produce cytokines like IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α, contributing to inflammation and joint destruction.

items.title: Key Mediators of Joint Inflammation in RA
items.content: Recent studies using synovial tissue samples have identified distinct cell populations driving joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis. These include IFN-γ-secreting granzyme K+ CD8+ T cells, peripheral helper CD4+ T cells, activated sublining fibroblasts, and proinflammatory monocytes.
keyPoints: Synovial tissue samples can be divided into subsets based on cell population abundance: myeloid cells, T cells, B cells, synovial fibroblasts, and endothelial cells.
Key mediators of joint inflammation include IFN-γ-secreting granzyme K+ CD8+ T cells, peripheral helper CD4+ T cells, activated sublining fibroblasts, and proinflammatory monocytes.

items.title: Mechanisms of Bone Erosion in RA
items.content: Bone erosion in rheumatoid arthritis is closely linked to osteoclast activation at the pannus site, driven by RANKL expression from stromal cells, synovial fibroblasts, and T cells. Osteoprotegerin (OPG) regulates this process by acting as a decoy receptor for RANKL.
keyPoints: RANKL stimulates osteoclast differentiation and bone resorption upon binding to its receptor RANK on osteoclast progenitors.
Osteoprotegerin (OPG) blocks osteoclast formation by acting as a decoy receptor for RANKL.
Cytokines such as TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6, and IL-17 promote osteoclastogenesis by increasing RANKL expression.
Osteoclasts secrete cathepsin K, which degrades bone matrix and contributes to bone loss.

items.title: Revised Criteria for Early RA Diagnosis
items.content: The 2010 ACR-EULAR criteria for diagnosing rheumatoid arthritis (RA) allow for earlier identification of patients who may benefit from early treatment. Unlike previous criteria, symptoms do not need to be present for more than six weeks, and the inclusion of serum ACPA testing enhances specificity.
keyPoints: 2010 criteria score: 0–10; ≥6 indicates definite RA
No requirement for symptoms >6 weeks
Inclusion of serum ACPA test increases diagnostic specificity
Approximately 75% of RA patients are seropositive (RF and/or ACPA positive)
Criteria serve to classify, not diagnose, and identify high likelihood of chronic disease

items.title: Differential Diagnosis Considerations for RA
items.content: RA must be differentiated from other inflammatory arthritides based on clinical course, joint involvement patterns, extrajoint manifestations, and specific laboratory findings. Conditions like primary Sjögren's syndrome, spondyloarthropathies, PMR, RS3PE, and hepatitis C-related arthropathy can mimic RA.
keyPoints: Primary Sjögren's: 50% RF positive; ANA and anti-Ro/SSA antibodies common
Spondyloarthropathies: Distinguished by sacroiliitis, psoriasis, or IBD presence
PMR vs. seronegative RA: PMR affects neck/shoulder girdle more than wrists/hands
RS3PE: Characterized by distal limb pitting edema; responds to low-dose prednisone
Hepatitis C arthropathy: May involve small joints, often RF positive but not ACPA

items.title: Role of Serological Tests in RA Diagnosis
items.content: Serological tests such as RF and ACPA are crucial for diagnosing RA. While about 75% of patients with RA are seropositive, the remaining 25% may be seronegative, complicating diagnosis. ACPA is more specific than RF for RA.
keyPoints: Seropositivity in RA: ~75% test positive for RF and/or ACPA
ACPA carries greater specificity for RA than RF
Seronegative RA can be challenging to diagnose, especially in elderly patients

items.title: Role of RF and Anti-CCP in Diagnosing RA
items.content: RF and anti-CCP antibodies are key serological markers for diagnosing rheumatoid arthritis (RA). While RF is present in 75% of RA patients, it lacks specificity as it can be found in other conditions. In contrast, anti-CCP has a high diagnostic specificity of about 95%, making it particularly useful in distinguishing RA from other arthritides.
keyPoints: RF is found in 75% of RA patients but also in other diseases and healthy individuals (1–5%).
Anti-CCP antibodies have a diagnostic specificity of approximately 95% for RA.
Testing both RF and anti-CCP can provide incremental value as some RA patients are positive for one but not the other.

items.title: Prognostic Value of Anti-CCP in RA
items.content: The presence of anti-CCP antibodies is not only diagnostic but also prognostic, indicating a higher likelihood of worse outcomes in RA patients. This makes testing for anti-CCP valuable beyond initial diagnosis.
keyPoints: Anti-CCP antibodies are useful for predicting poorer prognosis in RA.
The presence of RF or anti-CCP has prognostic significance.

items.title: Distinguishing Inflammatory Arthritis from Noninflammatory Conditions Using Synovial Fluid Analysis
items.content: Synovial fluid analysis is crucial for confirming inflammatory arthritis, such as rheumatoid arthritis (RA), by evaluating white blood cell counts and identifying the predominant presence of neutrophils. This helps differentiate it from noninflammatory conditions like osteoarthritis.
keyPoints: WBC count in RA synovial fluid: 5000-50,000 WBC/µL
Noninflammatory condition WBC count: <2000 WBC/µL
Neutrophils are the predominant cell type in inflammatory arthritis
Synovial fluid analysis helps exclude infection or crystal-induced arthritis
items.reference: Rheumatoid Arthritis · SYNOVIAL FLUID ANALYSIS | JOINT IMAGING

items.title: Utilizing Joint Imaging for Diagnosing and Monitoring Rheumatoid Arthritis
items.content: Joint imaging, including plain radiography, MRI, and ultrasound, plays a vital role in diagnosing RA and monitoring disease progression. Each modality offers unique advantages: X-rays show bony changes; MRI detects early soft tissue abnormalities; ultrasound identifies synovitis and erosions.
keyPoints: Plain radiographs reveal periarticular osteopenia, joint space loss, and erosions
MRI is sensitive for detecting synovitis, effusions, and bone marrow edema
Ultrasound detects more erosions than X-rays and assesses synovitis with power Doppler
Bone marrow edema on MRI predicts future erosions
items.reference: Rheumatoid Arthritis · SYNOVIAL FLUID ANALYSIS | JOINT IMAGING

items.title: Spontaneous Remission in RA: Identifying Candidates for Close Monitoring
items.content: Up to 10% of patients with inflammatory arthritis meeting ACR criteria for RA may experience spontaneous remission within 6 months, particularly those who are seronegative. This highlights the importance of monitoring these patients closely for changes in disease activity.
keyPoints: As many as 10% of patients with RA fulfilling ACR classification criteria undergo spontaneous remission within 6 months.
Spontaneous remissions are more common in seronegative patients.

items.title: Impact of RA on Mortality and Disability: Key Considerations for Management
items.content: RA significantly increases mortality rates, with a two-fold increase compared to the general population. The disease also leads to substantial disability over time, affecting employability and life expectancy.
keyPoints: Overall mortality rate in RA is twice that of the general population.
Median life expectancy is reduced by 7 years for men and 3 years for women with RA compared to controls.
More than half of patients are unable to work 10 years after disease onset, though newer therapies have improved employability.
Increased risk factors for shortened survival include systemic extraarticular involvement, low functional capacity, low socioeconomic status, low education, and chronic prednisone use.

items.title: Assessment and Management of Disease Activity in RA
items.content: The management of Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA) is heavily influenced by the assessment of clinical disease activity, which reflects inflammation burden. Composite indices like ACR 20, 50, 70 improvement criteria and continuous measures such as DAS, SDAI, CDAI, and RAPID3 are crucial for evaluating treatment response and guiding therapeutic decisions.
keyPoints: ACR 20, 50, 70 improvement criteria assess a 20%, 50%, 70% improvement in joint counts, disease severity, pain scale, serum levels of acute-phase reactants (ESR or CRP), and functional disability using patient questionnaires.
DAS, SDAI, CDAI, RAPID3 are continuous measures used for tracking disease status and treatment response in clinical practice.
Composite indices guide treatment decisions by reflecting the overall burden of inflammation.

items.title: Therapeutic Strategies in RA Management
items.content: The therapeutic landscape for Rheumatoid Arthritis has evolved with methotrexate as the first-choice DMARD, alongside novel biologicals and combination regimens. While some therapies may delay disease onset in preclinical stages, most patients require a tailored combination of DMARDs to manage fluctuating disease activity and drug-related toxicities.
keyPoints: Methotrexate is the first-choice DMARD for early RA treatment.
Novel biologicals can be used alone or with methotrexate for enhanced efficacy.
Combination DMARD regimens are often necessary due to disease activity fluctuations, loss of efficacy, and drug-related toxicities.

items.title: Role and Risks of NSAIDs in RA Management
items.content: NSAIDs are adjunctive agents for symptom management in rheumatoid arthritis (RA) when other treatments fail. They provide analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects by nonselectively inhibiting COX-1 and COX-2 enzymes. Despite their efficacy, chronic use should be minimized due to potential side effects.
keyPoints: NSAIDs are adjunctive in RA therapy for uncontrolled symptoms.
They inhibit both COX-1 and COX-2 nonselectively.
Chronic NSAID use risks include gastritis, peptic ulcer disease, and kidney injury.

items.title: Utilization of Glucocorticoids in RA Treatment
items.content: Glucocorticoids are used for rapid disease control before DMARDs take effect, managing acute flares with short bursts, and as a chronic low-dose therapy when DMARDs are insufficient. They should be minimized to avoid long-term complications like osteoporosis.
keyPoints: Low/moderate doses of glucocorticoids can rapidly control RA before DMARDs.
1-2 week bursts manage acute flares, with dose based on severity.
Chronic low-dose prednisone (5–10 mg/d) may be used if DMARDs are inadequate.
High-dose glucocorticoids treat severe extraarticular RA manifestations like ILD.
Intraarticular injections can control inflammation in few inflamed joints, with caution to exclude infection.
Chronic prednisone use increases osteoporosis risk; bisphosphonates or other agents may prevent it.

items.title: Methotrexate as the Anchor Drug for RA Treatment
items.content: Methotrexate is considered the cornerstone DMARD for rheumatoid arthritis (RA) due to its efficacy in slowing or preventing structural progression. It serves as the anchor drug in most combination therapies.
keyPoints: Methotrexate was approved for RA treatment in 1988 and remains a benchmark for newer therapies.
It has a delayed onset of action, typically around 6–12 weeks or longer.
Administered weekly by oral or subcutaneous route.
Folic acid is co-administered to mitigate side effects.
Stimulates adenosine release from cells, producing an anti-inflammatory effect.

items.title: Role and Limitations of Hydroxychloroquine in RA
items.content: Hydroxychloroquine is used for early, mild RA or as adjunctive therapy but does not delay radiographic progression, thus not considered a true DMARD.
keyPoints: Not shown to delay radiographic progression of RA.
Used for early, mild disease or in combination with other DMARDs.
Prescribed at 5 mg/kg body weight or less to decrease retinal toxicity risk.

items.title: Use of Anti-TNF Agents in RA
items.content: Anti-TNF agents, including infliximab, adalimumab, golimumab, certolizumab pegol, and etanercept, are used to reduce signs and symptoms of RA, slow joint damage progression, and improve quality of life. They can be used alone or with methotrexate.
keyPoints: TNF inhibitors target TNF-α, a critical mediator in joint inflammation.
Approved for RA treatment since 1999.
Can be combined with methotrexate for patients with inadequate response to methotrexate alone.
Contraindicated in active infection, hypersensitivity history, chronic hepatitis B, or class III/IV CHF.
Increased risk of serious infections and reactivation of latent TB; screening required before initiation.

items.title: Rituximab for Refractory RA
items.content: Rituximab, a chimeric monoclonal antibody against CD20, is used in refractory RA when TNF-α inhibitors fail. It depletes B cells and reduces inflammation through various mechanisms.
keyPoints: Approved for use with methotrexate in refractory RA.
More effective in seropositive patients.
Associated with mild to moderate infusion reactions and increased infection risk.
Rarely linked to progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) at ~1:25,000 risk.
Significantly inhibits vaccine response.

items.title: Abatacept's Role in RA Management
items.content: Abatacept, a fusion protein that blocks T-cell co-stimulation, reduces disease activity and slows joint damage. It is typically used with conventional DMARDs.
keyPoints: Inhibits CD28-CD80/86 interactions to reduce inflammation.
Associated with increased infection risk and vaccine response inhibition.

items.title: IL-6 Receptor Inhibitors: Efficacy and Risks in RA Treatment
items.content: Tocilizumab and sarilumab are monoclonal antibodies targeting IL-6 receptors, effective for RA treatment as monotherapy or with methotrexate. They carry risks of infection, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, increased LDL cholesterol, and gastrointestinal perforation.
keyPoints: IL-6 receptor inhibitors include tocilizumab and sarilumab.
Effective for RA treatment as monotherapy or with methotrexate.
Associated risks: infection, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, increased LDL cholesterol, gastrointestinal perforation.
items.reference: Rheumatoid Arthritis · BIOLOGICALS (part 2) | TARGETED SYNTHETIC DMARDs

items.title: JAK Inhibitors: Mechanism and Safety Profile in RA
items.content: JAK inhibitors like tofacitinib, baricitinib, and upadacitinib target intracellular signaling pathways. They are effective for RA but carry risks of infections, elevated liver enzymes, neutropenia, increased cholesterol, hypertension, serum creatinine elevation, thrombosis, cardiovascular events, and malignancies.
keyPoints: JAK inhibitors include tofacitinib (JAK1/JAK3), baricitinib (JAK1/JAK2), upadacitinib (selective JAK1).
Used as monotherapy or with methotrexate for RA treatment.
Risks: infections, elevated liver enzymes, neutropenia, increased cholesterol, hypertension, serum creatinine elevation, thrombosis, cardiovascular events, malignancies.

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF EXTRAARTICULAR MANIFESTATIONS | Rheumatoid Arthritis (part 1)
items.title: Approach to RA-ILD Treatment
items.content: RA-ILD treatment is challenging due to potential pulmonary toxicity from DMARDs like methotrexate and leflunomide. High doses of corticosteroids and immunosuppressive agents such as azathioprine, mycophenolate mofetil, or rituximab are often used.
keyPoints: RA-ILD treatment involves high-dose corticosteroids and immunosuppressants like azathioprine, mycophenolate mofetil, or rituximab.
DMARDs such as methotrexate and leflunomide can have pulmonary toxicity.
items.reference: Rheumatoid Arthritis · TREATMENT OF EXTRAARTICULAR MANIFESTATIONS | Rheumatoid Arthritis (part 1)

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF EXTRAARTICULAR MANIFESTATIONS | Rheumatoid Arthritis (part 1)
items.title: Principles of RA Treatment
items.content: RA treatment follows principles including early, aggressive therapy to prevent joint damage and disability; frequent modification of DMARD therapy; individualization of therapy; minimal long-term glucocorticoid use; and aiming for low disease activity or remission.
keyPoints: Early, aggressive therapy is crucial to prevent joint damage and disability.
Frequent modification of DMARD therapy is necessary to achieve treatment goals.
Treatment aims for low disease activity or clinical remission.
items.reference: Rheumatoid Arthritis · TREATMENT OF EXTRAARTICULAR MANIFESTATIONS | Rheumatoid Arthritis (part 1)

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF EXTRAARTICULAR MANIFESTATIONS | Rheumatoid Arthritis (part 1)
items.title: Escalation Strategy in RA Management
items.content: In early RA with persistent moderate/high disease activity despite DMARD monotherapy, escalation to combination DMARD therapy or switching to an anti-TNF +/- methotrexate or a non-TNF biologic +/- methotrexate is recommended. If no adequate response is seen after 3-6 months, step up to combination DMARDs or add a biological agent.
keyPoints: Escalation to combination DMARD therapy if moderate/high disease activity persists despite monotherapy.
Consider switching to an anti-TNF +/- methotrexate or non-TNF biologic +/- methotrexate in early RA.
Step up treatment after 3-6 months if no adequate response is achieved.
items.reference: Rheumatoid Arthritis · TREATMENT OF EXTRAARTICULAR MANIFESTATIONS | Rheumatoid Arthritis (part 1)

items.title: Defining Remission in Rheumatoid Arthritis
items.content: Remission in RA is defined by stringent criteria involving the absence of all articular and extraarticular inflammation. The ACR and EULAR have developed provisional operational definitions to standardize remission for clinical trials, utilizing composite indices like DAS-28 and SDAI scores.
keyPoints: Complete remission involves no detectable articular or extraarticular inflammation.
ACR and EULAR define remission as meeting specific criteria or having an SDAI score of <3.3.
SDAI is calculated from tender joint count, swollen joint count (28 joints), patient global assessment (0–10 scale), physician global assessment (0–10 scale), and CRP level in mg/dL.
Remission criteria aim to minimize structural damage progression and disability.

items.title: Treatment Strategy for Achieving Remission in RA
items.content: An early, aggressive treatment approach can lead a significant number of patients to remission with reduced therapy risk. However, withdrawing DMARDs during established disease typically results in relapse.
keyPoints: Most patients do not achieve complete remission despite efforts.
Early and aggressive treatment increases chances of reaching remission.
Patients in remission may reduce treatment without increased flare or loss of remission risk.
Complete withdrawal of DMARD therapy during established disease usually leads to relapse.

items.subtopic: PHYSICAL ACTIVITY AND ASSISTIVE DEVICES | SURGERY | OTHER MANAGEMENT CONSIDERATIONS
items.title: Exercise Prescription in RA: Benefits and Guidelines
items.content: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis (RA) should be prescribed exercise as part of their management plan. The American College of Rheumatology (ACR) guidelines recommend integrative interventions, including dynamic strength training, community-based physical therapy, and physical-activity coaching to achieve 150 minutes per week of moderate-to-vigorous activity. These activities improve muscle strength, perceived health status, DAS-28 scores, and inflammatory markers.
keyPoints: ACR recommends exercise as part of RA management.
Dynamic strength training, community-based physical therapy, and physical-activity coaching are beneficial.
Target: 150 minutes per week of moderate-to-vigorous activity.
Benefits include improved muscle strength, perceived health status, DAS-28 scores, and inflammatory markers.

items.subtopic: PHYSICAL ACTIVITY AND ASSISTIVE DEVICES | SURGERY | OTHER MANAGEMENT CONSIDERATIONS
items.title: Surgical Interventions in RA: Indications and Considerations
items.content: Surgery can improve pain and disability in RA, particularly for the hands, wrists, feet, and large joints like knees and hips. Total joint arthroplasty is preferred for advanced disease in large joints. For smaller hand joints, silicone implants are used for MCP arthroplasty in severe cases with marked flexion contractures and ulnar drift. Arthrodesis and total wrist arthroplasty are options for patients with severe pain and functional impairment.
keyPoints: Surgery can improve RA-related pain and disability.
Total joint arthroplasty is preferred for large joints in advanced disease.
Silicone implants for MCP arthroplasty in severe cases with marked flexion contractures and ulnar drift.
Arthrodesis and total wrist arthroplasty are options for severe hand joint pain and impairment.

items.title: Increased RA Incidence and Challenges in Developing Countries
items.content: In developing countries, there is an increase in non-communicable diseases like RA due to poverty, infectious disease prevalence, and poor healthcare access. This results in delayed diagnosis and limited specialist care, leading to higher disease activity and disability at presentation.
keyPoints: Higher incidence of chronic diseases such as RA
Delayed diagnosis and greater disease activity
Significant infection risk due to immunosuppression from treatments like glucocorticoids and DMARDs
Increased tuberculosis incidence in RA patients, necessitating comprehensive screening and isoniazid prophylaxis

items.title: Early Treatment of RA with Available Resources
items.content: Despite challenges, early treatment of RA should be initiated using accessible medications like hydroxychloroquine, sulfasalazine, and methotrexate. Biological agents are increasingly available but limited by cost and infection risk concerns.
keyPoints: Initiate early RA treatment with accessible drugs
Hydroxychloroquine, sulfasalazine, and methotrexate are globally accessible
Biological agents' use is restricted due to high costs and infection risks

items.title: Multidisciplinary Approach in RA Management
items.content: The management of RA requires early, aggressive treatment and frequent monitoring. A team approach involving primary care physicians and rheumatologists is essential for a treat-to-target strategy aiming for remission or low disease activity.
keyPoints: Early and aggressive RA treatment with frequent follow-up
Treat-to-target approach to achieve remission or low disease activity
Collaboration between primary care physicians and rheumatologists

items.question: Which autoantibodies are valuable in the diagnostic evaluation of patients with suspected rheumatoid arthritis?
options: Anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA) and rheumatoid factor
items.explanation: Testing for serum antibodies to anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA) and rheumatoid factor is valuable in the diagnostic evaluation of RA.

items.question: What is a major goal in the management of rheumatoid arthritis?
options: Achieving low disease activity and remission
Preventing any form of joint movement
items.explanation: Reaching a clinical state of low disease activity and remission is an achievable goal for most RA patients.

items.question: What has been a significant barrier to finding a cure for rheumatoid arthritis?
options: Incomplete understanding of the initiating events and factors perpetuating chronic inflammation
items.explanation: An incomplete understanding of the initiating events of RA and the factors perpetuating the chronic inflammatory response remains a barrier to finding a cure.

items.question: Which joints are typically the earliest to be involved in rheumatoid arthritis (RA)?
options: Proximal interphalangeal (PIP) and distal interphalangeal (DIP) joints
Small joints of the hands and feet
items.explanation: The earliest involved joints in RA are mostly the small joints of the hands and feet.

items.question: What is a common deformity caused by subluxation of the MCP joints in long-standing RA?
items.explanation: Ulnar deviation is a common deformity caused by subluxation of the MCP joints in long-standing RA.

items.question: Which extraarticular manifestation is most likely to develop in patients with a history of cigarette smoking and positive serum RF or ACPA?
items.explanation: Patients with a history of cigarette smoking and positive serum RF or ACPA are more likely to develop extraarticular manifestations such as subcutaneous nodules.

items.question: What is the typical duration of early morning joint stiffness in patients presenting with RA?
items.explanation: Patients with RA typically experience early morning joint stiffness lasting >1 hour that eases with physical activity.

items.question: Which of the following is a constitutional symptom that may precede joint symptoms in rheumatoid arthritis?
items.explanation: Constitutional symptoms such as weight loss, fever, and fatigue can precede joint symptoms in rheumatoid arthritis.

items.explanation: Approximately 10% of patients with rheumatoid arthritis have secondary Sjögren's syndrome.

items.explanation: Pleuritis is the most common pulmonary manifestation of rheumatoid arthritis.

items.explanation: High-resolution chest CT scans show ground-glass opacities as a characteristic finding in ILD associated with RA.

items.question: Which cardiac manifestation in rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is the most frequent site of involvement?
items.explanation: The pericardium is the most frequent site of cardiac involvement in RA.

items.question: What percentage of patients with rheumatoid arthritis may have asymptomatic pericardial effusions detectable by echocardiography?
items.explanation: Up to 20% of patients with RA may have asymptomatic pericardial effusions on echocardiography.

items.question: Which hematologic abnormality is most common in rheumatoid arthritis?
items.explanation: A normochromic, normocytic anemia is the most common hematologic abnormality in RA.

items.question: Which type of lymphoma is most commonly associated with rheumatoid arthritis (RA)?
items.explanation: The most common histopathologic type of lymphoma in RA patients is diffuse large B-cell lymphoma.

items.question: What increases the risk of developing lymphoma in rheumatoid arthritis (RA) patients?
options: High levels of disease activity or presence of Felty's syndrome

items.question: What is the most common cause of death in patients with rheumatoid arthritis (RA)?
items.explanation: The most common cause of death in patients with RA is cardiovascular disease.

items.question: Which gene is most strongly associated with an increased risk of rheumatoid arthritis (RA)?
items.explanation: The HLA-DRB1 gene, particularly its alleles encoding the MHC class II beta-chain molecule, is most strongly associated with RA risk.

items.question: What is the term for the shared amino acid sequence at positions 70–74 in HLA-DRB1 alleles that increases RA risk?
items.explanation: The shared amino acid sequence at positions 70–74 in HLA-DRB1 alleles is termed the 'shared epitope' (SE).

items.question: Which of the following statements about geographic variation in RA susceptibility is correct?
options: The '0401 SE allele is predominant in Asian populations.
The '0101 SE allele is associated with milder RA in Greece.
HLA-DRB1 '1302 confers a high risk of ACPA-positive RA.
RA susceptibility is not influenced by geographic variation.
items.explanation: '0101 SE allele is associated with milder RA in Greece, while '0401 or '0404 are predominant in northern Europeans.

items.question: What percentage of the genetic risk for RA do non-MHC loci identified by GWAS account for?
items.explanation: Non-MHC loci identified by GWAS account for approximately 5% of the genetic risk for RA.

items.question: Which non-MHC gene is associated with a gain-of-function leading to abnormal thymic selection of autoreactive T and B cells in RA?
items.explanation: Inheritance of the risk allele for PTPN22 produces a gain-of-function in the protein, leading to abnormal thymic selection.

items.question: Which gene's polymorphism is associated with a twofold increase in RA risk primarily among East Asian populations?
items.explanation: A polymorphism in PADI4 has been associated with a twofold increase in the risk of RA, primarily in those of East Asian descent.

items.question: What epigenetic mechanism is involved in regulating posttranslational histone modifications and DNA methylation in RA?
items.explanation: The best-studied epigenetic mechanisms are those regulating posttranslational histone modifications and DNA methylation.

items.question: Which environmental factor has been shown to interact synergistically with MHC risk alleles to increase the likelihood of developing RA?
items.explanation: Smoking interacts synergistically with MHC risk alleles to significantly increase RA risk.

items.question: What is the approximate increased risk of developing RA for women who smoke cigarettes?
items.explanation: Women who smoke have a nearly 2.5 times greater risk of developing RA.

items.question: Which bacterial enzyme is hypothesized to induce ACPA by catalyzing the citrullination of arginine residues?
items.explanation: The enzyme peptidyl arginine deiminase (PAD) is hypothesized to induce ACPA.

items.question: Which oral bacterial species is known to harbor the enzyme that may trigger RA development?
items.explanation: Porphyromonas gingivalis is the only oral bacterial species known to harbor peptidyl arginine deiminase (PAD).

items.question: Which cell types are primarily found in the synovial membrane of joints affected by RA?
options: Type A and Type B synoviocytes, macrophages, and fibroblasts
T cells, B cells, plasma cells, dendritic cells, mast cells, and granulocytes
items.explanation: The synovial membrane primarily consists of type A synoviocytes (macrophage-derived) and type B synoviocytes (fibroblast-derived).

items.question: What is the main function of hyaluronan in synovial fluid?
options: To provide structural support to the joint
To contribute to the viscous nature of synovial fluid
To promote inflammation within the joint
items.explanation: Hyaluronan is a glycosaminoglycan that contributes to the viscous nature of synovial fluid.

items.question: Where do bone erosions typically develop in RA?
options: At the center of articular cartilage
Along the metaphyses of bones from inflammation of the bone marrow cavity
At radial sites of MCP joints juxtaposed to insertion sites of tendons and ligaments
In regions far from synovial pannus
items.explanation: Bone erosions typically develop at the radial sites of the MCP joints where the synovial membrane inserts into periosteal surfaces.

items.question: What characterizes the initial areas of cartilage degradation in RA?
options: Loss of chondrocytes and increased bone formation
Generalized loss of proteoglycan, especially in superficial zones
Increased collagen production and thickening of cartilage
items.explanation: The initial areas of cartilage degradation are characterized by a generalized loss of proteoglycan, most evident in the superficial zones adjacent to the synovial fluid.

items.question: Which enzyme is responsible for the posttranslational modification that results in deaminated peptides recognized by ACPAs?
items.explanation: ACPA recognizes citrulline-containing regions of several different matrix proteins due to posttranslational modification by the enzyme PADI4.

items.question: What is a speculated environmental trigger that might induce citrullination and lead to RA?
items.explanation: Long-term exposure to tobacco smoke might induce citrullination of cellular proteins, leading to self-reactivity and joint inflammation.

items.question: Which Toll-like receptors are abundantly expressed by synovial fibroblasts in early RA?
items.explanation: In early RA, synovial fibroblasts abundantly express TLR2, TLR3, and TLR4.

items.question: What role do CD4+ T cells play in the pathogenesis of RA?
options: They stabilize MHC-peptide-T-cell receptor complex and orchestrate chronic inflammatory response
items.explanation: CD4+ T cells bind to invariant sites on MHC class II molecules, stabilizing the MHC-peptide-T-cell receptor complex during activation, and play a role in orchestrating the chronic inflammatory response.

items.question: Which cytokine is primarily produced by T helper 17 (T_H17) cells?
items.explanation: T_H17 cells secrete a variety of proinflammatory mediators such as IL-17.

items.question: What is the role of TNF-α in rheumatoid arthritis?
options: It suppresses inflammation by inhibiting cytokine production.
It upregulates adhesion molecules on endothelial cells, promoting leukocyte influx.
It directly destroys cartilage and bone without other mediators.
It induces T cell anergy to prevent autoimmune responses.
items.explanation: TNF-α upregulates adhesion molecules on endothelial cells, promoting the influx of leukocytes into the synovial microenvironment.

items.question: Which cell type is considered a predominant source of proinflammatory cytokines in rheumatoid arthritis?
items.explanation: RA is often considered to be a macrophage-driven disease because this cell type is the predominant source of proinflammatory cytokines inside the joint.

items.question: What signaling pathways are involved in amplifying the inflammatory response in rheumatoid arthritis?
items.explanation: Examples of signaling molecules in these critical inflammatory pathways include Janus kinase (JAK)/signal transducers and activators of transcription (STAT), spleen tyrosine kinase (Syk), mitogen-activated protein kinases (MAPKs), and nuclear factor-κB (NF-κB).

items.question: Which cell types are identified as key mediators of joint inflammation in RA according to recent studies?
options: IFN-γ-secreting granzyme K+ CD8+ T cells, peripheral helper CD4+ T cells, activated sublining fibroblasts, and proinflammatory monocytes
B cells, endothelial cells, and synovial fibroblasts
items.explanation: These cell types are highlighted as key mediators of joint inflammation in RA.

items.question: What regulates RANKL activity to prevent osteoclast formation?
items.explanation: OPG acts as a decoy receptor for RANKL, blocking osteoclast formation.

items.question: Which cytokine is primarily responsible for suppressing bone formation in RA by enhancing the expression of DKK-1?
items.explanation: TNF-α enhances the expression of DKK-1, which inhibits bone formation.

items.question: Which of the following criteria are included in the 2010 ACR-EULAR classification for RA?
options: Symptoms must be present for more than 6 weeks.
A positive test for serum ACPA is included as an item.
Presence of rheumatoid nodules or radiographic joint damage are required items.
The criteria serve to distinguish patients at the onset who have a high likelihood of evolving to chronic disease.
items.explanation: The 2010 ACR-EULAR criteria include a positive test for serum ACPA, which is more specific for RA than RF.

items.question: What is the minimum score required in the 2010 ACR-EULAR classification criteria to fulfill the requirements for definite RA?
items.explanation: A score of ≥6 is needed to meet the criteria for a diagnosis of definite RA.

items.question: Which antibody test carries greater specificity for diagnosing RA according to the 2010 ACR-EULAR classification?
items.explanation: Serum ACPA has greater specificity for RA compared to RF.

items.question: Which condition is characterized by prominent distal limb pitting edema and responds promptly to low doses of prednisone?
options: Remitting seronegative symmetrical synovitis with pitting edema (RS3PE)
items.explanation: RS3PE is characterized by prominent distal limb pitting edema and responds to low doses of prednisone.

items.question: Which antibody has a diagnostic specificity approaching 95% for rheumatoid arthritis (RA)?
items.explanation: The presence of serum anti-CCP antibodies has about the same sensitivity as serum RF for the diagnosis of RA, but its diagnostic specificity approaches 95%.

items.question: Which antibody is most frequently measured by commercial laboratories in patients with rheumatoid arthritis (RA)?
items.explanation: The IgM isotype of RF occurs most frequently in sera from patients with RA and is the one most frequently measured by commercial laboratories.

items.question: What percentage of healthy individuals may have low levels of serum RF detected?
items.explanation: Serum RF, mostly at low levels, may also be detected in 1–5% of the healthy population.

items.question: What is the typical range of white blood cell (WBC) counts in synovial fluid from patients with rheumatoid arthritis (RA)?
items.explanation: The typical range of WBC counts in synovial fluid from RA patients is between 5000 and 50,000 WBC/µL.

items.question: Which index is NOT a continuous measure used in clinical practice to track disease status and document treatment response?
items.explanation: The ACR improvement criteria is not a continuous measure; it has a dichotomous response variable.

items.question: Which enzyme do NSAIDs nonselectively inhibit to exert their anti-inflammatory effects?
items.explanation: NSAIDs inhibit cyclooxygenase enzymes, specifically COX-1 and COX-2, to reduce inflammation.

items.question: What is a potential long-term complication of chronic prednisone use?
items.explanation: Chronic use of prednisone can lead to osteoporosis, a significant long-term complication.

items.question: When might intraarticular injection of glucocorticoids be considered in RA management?
options: In patients with multiple joint infections
To rapidly control inflammation in a few affected joints
As first-line treatment for all RA patients
items.explanation: Intraarticular glucocorticoid injections are used to quickly manage inflammation in a limited number of inflamed joints.

items.question: Which DMARD is considered the anchor drug for most combination therapies in the treatment of RA?
items.explanation: Methotrexate is the DMARD of choice and anchor drug for most combination therapies in RA.

items.question: Which DMARD has not been shown to delay radiographic progression of disease, thus not considered a true DMARD?
items.explanation: Hydroxychloroquine has not been shown to delay radiographic progression of disease.

items.question: What is the mechanism by which methotrexate exerts an anti-inflammatory effect at RA treatment dosages?
options: Stimulation of adenosine release from cells
items.explanation: Methotrexate stimulates adenosine release from cells, producing an anti-inflammatory effect.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT an anti-TNF monoclonal antibody approved for RA treatment?

items.question: Which biological DMARD targets CD20 on B lymphocytes?

items.question: Which of the following anti-TNF agents is a pegylated Fab' fragment?

items.question: Which biological DMARD inhibits T-cell co-stimulation by blocking CD28-CD80/86 interactions?

items.question: Which agents are monoclonal antibodies directed against both membrane and soluble forms of the IL-6 receptor?
items.explanation: Tocilizumab and sarilumab are monoclonal antibodies targeting the IL-6 receptor.

Infection risk due to immunosuppression from glucocorticoids and DMARDs.
Lack of understanding of RA pathogenesis.
